perturbation.original_text: ALL DISPUTES ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE SETTLED VIA BINDING ARBITRATION ACCORDING TO THE RULES AND REGULATIONS SET FORTH BY THE AMERICAN ARBITRATION ASSOCIATION IN SAN DIEGO COUNTY , THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL DISPUTES ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE SETTLED VIA BINDING ARBITRATION.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specification of arbitration rules and location (San Diego County, CA) could contradict California law which sets specific standards for arbitration agreements, including disclosures and fairness requirements. Omitting the location and specific rules could allow the arbitration to occur in a less consumer-friendly jurisdiction or under rules that are less favorable to consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.97 requires specific disclosures and standards for neutral arbitrators. By omitting the reference to AAA rules and San Diego County, the agreement becomes vague and potentially unenforceable under California law because it doesn't ensure compliance with these arbitrator disclosure requirements, especially regarding potential bias.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281.97 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.97. (a) (1) In an employment or consumer arbitration that requires, either expressly or through application of state or federal law or the rules of the arbitration provider, the drafting party to pay certain fees and costs before the arbitration can proceed, if the fees or costs to initiate an arbitration proceeding are not paid within 30 days after the due date the drafting party is in material breach of the arbitration agreement, is in default of the arbitration, and waives its right to compel arbitration under Section 1281.2. (2) After an employee or consumer meets the filing requirements necessary to initiate an arbitration, the arbitration provider shall immediately provide an invoice for any fees and costs required before the arbitration can proceed to all of the parties to the arbitration. The invoice shall be provided in its entirety, shall state the full amount owed and the date that payment is due, and shall be sent to all parties b
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific reference to AAA rules and the location of arbitration (San Diego County, CA). The Law Explanation states that California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.97 requires specific disclosures and standards for neutral arbitrators. Omitting the reference to AAA rules and San Diego County could make the agreement vague and potentially unenforceable under California law because it doesn't ensure compliance with arbitrator disclosure requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1281.97 (a)(1), discusses the requirements for fees and costs in employment or consumer arbitration, indicating specific legal requirements that the modified text potentially jeopardizes by removing location and rule specifications.

perturbation.original_text: PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that the PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content is available at the request of THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION.
perturbation.changed_text: PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT will ensure that the PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content may be available at the request of THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable best efforts to ensure' to 'ensure that the PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content may be available' significantly weakens the obligation of PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the guarantee of content availability. If the content is unavailable, it impacts the licensee's ability to create content, which diminishes the value of the license. This could be interpreted as a breach of contract under California law, particularly the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, if the lack of availability undermines the contract's purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2306(1) states that a term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. By weakening PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT'S obligation, it makes it more likely that the quantity of content actually delivered will be unreasonably disproportionate to what THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION reasonably expected, thereby breaching the implied covenant of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2306. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2306. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT to use "reasonable best efforts" to ensure content availability, which implies a strong obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to "ensure that the PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content may be available." This shift introduces ambiguity and reduces the guarantee of content availability. While the Law Explanation refers to California Commercial Code § 2306(1) regarding quantity and good faith, the core contradiction lies in the dilution of PACIFICAP's obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] 2306. (2) states: "A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale." This snippet supports that 'best efforts' is an obligation, and weakening this to 'may be available' weakens the obligation and thus creates a contradiction, by not meeting the standard of "best efforts."

perturbation.original_text: THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION agrees to use its best efforts to restrict the uses of PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content by visitors to its Web Pages to personal use of such Content and not for further commercial redistribution.
perturbation.changed_text: THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION agrees to restrict the uses of PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT Content by visitors to its Web Pages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'to personal use of such Content and not for further commercial redistribution' creates a legal contradiction. Without that specification, THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION would only have to restrict uses in general, which might not be legally sufficient. California's Business and Professions Code prohibits unfair competition, which includes misusing licensed content for unauthorized commercial redistribution. The omission weakens consumer protection by allowing potential commercial misuse, in contradiction to California Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. By removing the explicit restriction against commercial redistribution, the contract increases the risk that THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION, even inadvertently, engages in practices that constitute unfair competition. For example, if visitors commercially redistribute content, and THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION did not take reasonable measures to prevent it, that could be construed as facilitating an unfair business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase "to personal use of such Content and not for further commercial redistribution" from the original text introduces a legal contradiction. California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice. By removing the explicit restriction, the changed text increases the risk of facilitating unfair competition if visitors commercially redistribute content, as THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION may not take reasonable measures to prevent it. This directly undermines the consumer protection against unauthorized commercial redistribution, contradicting the intent of BPC 17200, as it opens a loophole for potential misuse that the original text explicitly prevented.
